1: "This is the word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD when King Zedekiah sent Pashhur son of Malkijah and the priest Zephaniah son of Maaseiah to him." The royal delegation to Jeremiah at the crisis point of the Babylonian siege (588 BCE) marks the transition from the Confessions (Jeremiah 11-20) to the royal oracles (Jeremiah 21-24). The same establishment that persecuted Jeremiah now seeks his prophetic counsel. Christologically, this pattern of seeking the prophet's word in crisis after rejecting it in peace is the pattern Jesus identifies: "You will not see me until you say, 'Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord'" (Matthew 23:39) — the recognition that comes too late.
2: "Please seek a word from the LORD for us, because Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon is attacking us. Perhaps the LORD will do something miraculous for us." Zedekiah's request invokes the môpēt (miracle, wonder) pattern of the exodus — "perhaps YHWH will do one of his wonders" — in hopes of a Hezekiah-style deliverance (2 Kings 19:35). Christologically, this demand for a miraculous sign in crisis is the pattern Jesus refuses: "An evil and adulterous generation seeks a sign" (Matthew 12:39). The miracle requested is the wrong question; the right question is whether the asker has responded to the prophetic word that came in peace.
3: "Jeremiah answered them: Tell Zedekiah this" — the prophet's response is not the hoped-for assurance but the word of judgment. The prophetic daring required to deliver an oracle of doom to the besieged king mirrors the boldness Christ displays before Pilate (John 18:37: "You say that I am a king. For this purpose I was born and for this purpose I have come into the world — to bear witness to the truth"). Both Jeremiah before Zedekiah and Jesus before Pilate speak an uncomfortable truth rather than a comfortable falsehood.
4: "I am going to turn back the weapons in your hands — the very weapons with which you are fighting the king of Babylon." The grotesque reversal: YHWH will redirect Judah's own weapons against herself. The military equipment meant for defense becomes the instrument of self-destruction through divine sovereign redirection. Christologically, the cross enacts the same reversal: the powers that executed Jesus used the instruments of death (the cross) only to find that the very death they inflicted became the instrument of their own defeat (Colossians 2:15: "He disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him").
5: "And I myself will fight against you with an outstretched hand and a mighty arm, in burning anger and fury and great wrath." The exodus formula inverted: the "outstretched arm and mighty hand" that delivered Israel from Egypt (Deuteronomy 4:34; Jeremiah 5:15; Jeremiah 7:19; Jeremiah 26:8) is now turned against Israel. Christologically, the inverted-exodus formula is the OT form of divine wrath that Christ absorbs on behalf of the new covenant people. Galatians 3:13 ("Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us") means that Christ takes the divine wrath that the inverted exodus formulas represent — so that for those in Christ, the formula is re-inverted: the mighty arm that was against us is now for us (Romans 8:31: "if God is for us, who can be against us?").
6: "I will strike down everyone living in this city — both people and animals — and they will die from a terrible plague." The covenant-plague vocabulary (deber) is the Deuteronomic curse-fulfillment (Deuteronomy 28:21-22). The comprehensive judgment — people and animals alike — echoes the totality of judgment in the exodus plagues. Christologically, the totality of judgment here is what Christ's atonement addresses: not partial sin-coverage but comprehensive bearing of the curse that extends through the entire created order (Romans 8:19-22: "the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption").
7: "After that... I will hand over Zedekiah king of Judah, his officials, and those who survive... to Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, to their enemies who seek their lives. He will put them to the sword without pity, without mercy, without compassion." The threefold negation — no pity, no mercy, no compassion — is the polar opposite of the divine character of Exodus 34:6-7. Christologically, this absence of divine compassion in judgment is what Christ absorbs in his passion: the cross is the place where divine mercy and divine wrath meet, where pity, mercy, and compassion operate precisely by placing their cost on the innocent one.
8: "I am setting before you the way of life and the way of death." The Deuteronomic two-ways formula (Deuteronomy 30:15-19: "I set before you today life and prosperity, death and destruction") is applied to the immediate crisis: surrender to Babylon (counterintuitive life) or resist (apparent strength leading to death). Christologically, Jesus explicitly takes up the two-ways formula and applies it to himself: "I am the way, the truth, and the life; no one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). The Jeremianic "way of life" is an external path to follow; Christ IS the way, the life itself. John 10:10 ("I came that they may have life and have it abundantly") is the definitive statement of what the Deuteronomic and Jeremianic two-ways formula always pointed toward.
9: "Whoever stays in this city will die by the sword, famine, or plague; but whoever goes out and surrenders to the Chaldeans who besiege you will live — his life will be his prize." The counterintuitive way of life — surrendering to the enemy as the path to survival — requires trust in the prophetic word against all human instinct. Christologically, this is the pattern of the cross: "For whoever would save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life for my sake will find it" (Matthew 16:25). Surrendering to the Chaldean enemy to live is the OT type of surrendering oneself to the apparent defeat of the cross to gain resurrection life. The nefesh lĕšallāl (life as plunder/prize) of the one who surrenders is the OT image of the eternal life gained through faith in the crucified one.
10: "For I have fixed my gaze on this city for disaster and not for good, declares the LORD." The divine fixing of the gaze (śamtî pānay, I have set my face) for disaster is the reverse of the priestly blessing (Numbers 6:25: "the LORD make his face shine on you and be gracious to you"). Christologically, the divine face set against Jerusalem is turned from its object and toward Christ at the cross — who receives the curse in himself so that the face of divine grace can turn toward those who were under judgment. Isaiah 53:3 ("he was despised... as one from whom men hide their faces") is the servant's experience of the hidden divine face that Jeremiah 21:10 threatened.
11: "And to the house of the king of Judah, say: Hear the word of the LORD." The transitional verse addresses the Davidic royal house specifically. Christologically, the address to "the house of the king of Judah" is the address to the lineage from which Christ descends (Matthew 1:1-17; Luke 1:32-33: "the Lord God will give to him the throne of his father David"). The prophetic word to the Davidic house reaches its fulfillment in the one born in David's city (Luke 2:11) who is himself the definitive heir of David's throne.
12: "House of David! This is what the LORD says: Administer justice every morning; rescue from the oppressor those who have been robbed, or my anger will break out like an unquenchable fire." The justice-standard addressed to the house of David — daily morning justice for the oppressed — is the covenant calling of the Davidic king. Christologically, this is the Davidic standard that only Christ fully meets: he is the one who "proclaims justice to the nations" (Matthew 12:18-21, citing Isaiah 42:1-4), who "has been anointed... to proclaim good news to the poor, to set at liberty those who are oppressed" (Luke 4:18, citing Isaiah 61:1-2). Jesus's morning teaching in the temple (Luke 21:37-38: "every day he was teaching in the temple") is the fulfillment of the "every morning" standard of Davidic justice.
13: "I am against you, Jerusalem — you who sit enthroned in the valley, on your rocky plateau... who say, 'Who can attack us? Who can breach our strongholds?'" The pride of Jerusalem — trusting in her geographic and architectural defenses — is the self-confidence that YHWH opposes. Christologically, this is the form of institutional pride that Jesus confronts in his temple-cleansing and in his prediction of the temple's destruction (Matthew 24:1-2). The "Who can breach our strongholds?" boast of the Jerusalem establishment in Jeremiah's day is the same boast implicit in the claim that the temple and its institutions are inviolable — a claim that both Jeremiah and Jesus reject.
14: "I will punish you as your deeds deserve, declares the LORD. I will set fire to your forest — and it will burn up everything around you." The concluding judgment — fire consuming the palace-forest (yaʿar may refer to the House of the Forest of Lebanon, the royal armoury of 1 Kings 7:2) — is the OT form of the judicial fire that consumes what does not endure. Christologically, 1 Corinthians 3:13-15 uses the same "fire" imagery for the testing of human work on the day of judgment. But more immediately, the fire that burns Jerusalem's palace-forest is the fire that Jesus says he came to cast on the earth (Luke 12:49) — the purifying, consuming fire of divine judgment that the mission of Christ brings and that is the ultimate grounds for both the cross (where Christ absorbs the fire) and the final judgment (where all things are tested by fire).
